entries.title: Certain stock purchases treated as asset acquisitions
entries.body: Section 338 is the central Code provision for testing whether a nominal stock acquisition can be modeled as a deemed asset acquisition. For the current wedge, it is the threshold authority for asking whether the buyer can obtain asset-style basis results without abandoning stock form. It matters because the election is not just a pricing question: the parties first need a qualified stock purchase and an ownership profile that actually permits the election mechanics. It also sharpens the buyer-seller tradeoff. Buyers focus on basis step-up, amortization, and future gain-shielding. Sellers focus on whether deemed sale treatment creates immediate tax cost that a straight stock sale might avoid.

entries.title: Special allocation rules for certain asset acquisitions
entries.body: Section 1060 governs purchase price allocation in applicable asset acquisitions and therefore drives one of the most practical buyer-versus-seller questions in the wedge: how much value actually sits in basis step-up and where does that basis land. The provision matters in direct asset deals and in deemed asset transactions because the allocation mechanics determine depreciation, amortization, and character consequences. When a buyer says an asset path is preferable, Section 1060 helps test whether the expected basis value is real enough to justify seller tax cost, allocation friction, and transfer-tax exposure.

entries.title: Corporate reorganization nonrecognition framework
entries.body: Sections 354, 356, 361, and 368 remain relevant to the stock-versus-asset wedge because rollover equity and mixed consideration can move a transaction out of a simple taxable comparison and into reorganization-sensitive territory. These provisions are important when the parties want some stock-form continuity and are comparing that path against either a taxable stock acquisition or an asset-style structure. In this wedge, they help answer whether the retained equity is meaningful enough to support continuity-sensitive treatment and whether boot or sequencing changes the comparison.

entries.title: Limitation on net operating loss carryforwards and certain built-in items after ownership change
entries.body: Section 382 is the core attribute-preservation authority for the wedge when the buyer is comparing stock form against asset or deemed asset alternatives. A straight stock acquisition may preserve the entity and its history, but that does not mean the target's losses or other attributes remain fully usable. Section 382 therefore sits directly inside the buyer-side tradeoff: if the buyer values inherited attributes, the ownership-change limitations must be modeled alongside any basis-step-up alternative. If those attributes are already limited or fragile, a stock path can look less attractive relative to an asset-style result.

entries.title: Treatment of certain built-in gains and preacquisition losses
entries.body: Section 384 matters where the stock-versus-asset decision turns in part on how preacquisition losses can or cannot shelter later gain. It gives the wedge more nuance than a simple Section 382 flag because the question is not only whether attributes survive, but whether they can be used against the income streams the buyer expects after closing. That makes Section 384 particularly relevant when the buyer is considering keeping stock form but also expects integration steps, asset sales, or other post-acquisition gain events.

entries.title: Qualified stock purchase and general Section 338 election mechanics
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-1 provides the threshold operational rules for whether a Section 338 election can actually happen. For this wedge, it is one of the most valuable gating authorities because it moves the analysis from abstract step-up economics to election availability. If the buyer does not have a qualified stock purchase or the transaction does not fit the election mechanics, the deemed asset path is not a real recommendation, no matter how attractive the step-up may look in a model.

entries.title: Special rules for elections under Section 338(h)(10)
entries.body: Regulation 1.338(h)(10)-1 is the operational source for one of the most realistic election alternatives in the wedge. It matters because it ties the buyer's desire for asset-style basis results to concrete ownership, election, and seller-consent requirements. In practice, this regulation often determines whether a stock deal can be re-priced into a deemed asset result, whether a seller gross-up conversation is unavoidable, and whether the election path is truly available rather than merely tax-efficient in theory.

entries.title: Residual method allocation rules for applicable asset acquisitions
entries.body: Regulation 1.1060-1 supplies the operational allocation rules that make or break the value of an asset path. For this wedge, it matters because the buyer's claimed step-up benefit is only as good as the residual-method allocation that can be supported and documented. The regulation also sharpens seller-side cost analysis because allocation disputes can shift value across ordinary-income, capital-gain, and amortizable buckets. A stock-versus-asset recommendation is incomplete if the modeled step-up ignores these allocation mechanics.

entries.title: Continuity and business purpose rules for reorganizations
entries.body: Regulation 1.368-1 provides the continuity and business-purpose framework that matters whenever the wedge overlaps with a rollover or reorganization-sensitive structure. It helps distinguish between a taxable stock acquisition, a continuity-sensitive merger, and a path where the retained equity is too thin or too synthetic to support reorganization treatment. That overlap matters because the buyer and seller may compare a taxable stock path not only against an asset path, but also against a continuity-based merger alternative.

entries.title: Triangular merger rules relevant to stock-form acquisition structures
entries.body: Section 1.368-2(k) matters when the stock-versus-asset wedge is being tested inside a merger-sub structure. It is especially useful where the buyer wants stock-form execution with parent equity or mixed consideration but still needs the structure to stay inside reorganization-sensitive rules. In this wedge, it helps distinguish a true stock-form merger path from a taxable stock acquisition or asset-style result by forcing the analysis back to form, consideration, and sequencing details.

entries.title: Debt modification rules relevant to post-closing refinancing
entries.body: Regulation 1.1001-3 is relevant to the current wedge only where post-closing refinancing or debt amendments materially affect the stock-versus-asset comparison. It does not drive the structure question by itself, but it can change the modeled economics if the selected path depends on refinancing, seller notes, or significant debt amendments shortly after closing. That makes it a directly relevant financing overlay rather than a general financing authority.

entries.title: Classification of LLC acquisition structures as asset or stock-style transactions
entries.body: Revenue Ruling 99-6 is a practical IRS source for classifying certain LLC acquisition structures as either asset acquisitions or equity-style transactions. It is useful in this wedge because it helps prevent a superficial stock-versus-asset comparison from ignoring entity-classification consequences. Where the target or an acquisition vehicle is an LLC or disregarded entity, this ruling can materially change how buyer basis, seller gain, and election alternatives should be modeled.

entries.title: Integrated acquisition steps treated as a reorganization
entries.body: Revenue Ruling 2001-46 helps where the stock-versus-asset wedge overlaps with merger sequencing and step integration. It is useful when the parties are comparing a taxable stock path against a merger path that includes rollover equity or integrated steps. The ruling does not replace the Code and regulations, but it gives practical IRS guidance on how linked steps can still be respected as part of a reorganization-sensitive structure.

entries.title: Guidance on recognized built-in gains and losses under Section 382
entries.body: Notice 2003-65 is useful when the stock path is being valued partly on the expected usefulness of target attributes after an ownership change. It adds practical detail to the attribute-preservation overlap by addressing recognized built-in gain and loss approaches under Section 382. In the wedge, it helps distinguish between a stock deal that preserves meaningful tax value and one where the expected attribute upside is thinner than the headline numbers suggest.

entries.title: Asset Acquisition Statement Under Section 1060
entries.body: Form 8594 is the reporting form that operationalizes the Section 1060 allocation work. It is not the legal rule itself, but it is highly useful for this wedge because it forces the buyer and seller to commit to a reported allocation and exposes whether the modeled basis step-up is supported by a coherent residual-method story. That makes it a practical bridge between structure selection and actual reporting discipline.

entries.title: Asset Allocation Statement Under Section 338
entries.body: Form 8883 is important in the deemed-asset-election wedge because it translates Section 338 modeling into a concrete allocation statement. It helps the team test whether the election path is operationally coherent, whether the deemed sale and new-basis consequences have actually been modeled, and whether the allocation economics justify the election. It is therefore a practical companion to the Code and regulations rather than a substitute for them.

entries.title: Elections Under Section 338 for Corporations Making Qualified Stock Purchases
entries.body: Form 8023 is a practical election-mechanics source for the stock-versus-deemed-asset wedge. It matters because it forces the analysis to ask whether the transaction is truly capable of supporting a Section 338 election and whether the parties are prepared to make and document that choice on time. In the workflow, it is a useful operational check on whether a deemed asset path is real rather than merely a conceptual alternative.

entries.title: Step transaction treatment in acquisitive reorganizations
entries.citation: King Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 418 F.2d 511 (Ct. Cl. 1969)
entries.body: King Enterprises remains useful where the wedge overlaps with reorganization-sensitive sequencing rather than a clean taxable stock acquisition. It helps evaluate whether formally separate steps should be integrated when the parties are comparing a merger path against taxable stock or asset alternatives. For this wedge, it is best used as a narrow overlap authority rather than the main driver of the stock-versus-asset comparison.

entries.title: Debt-like consideration and continuity limits in acquisition structures
entries.body: LeTulle is useful when the wedge overlaps with rollover structures that may be too debt-like or too protected to support continuity-sensitive treatment. It is not a stock-versus-asset authority by itself, but it becomes relevant where the comparison includes a stock-form or merger path with seller equity that might actually behave more like debt. In that setting, it helps explain what facts would push the recommendation away from a rollover-sensitive path and toward a plainly taxable stock or asset result.
